_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Leflore County, MS. County seat: Greenwood. Records survive from county formation (1871) with no major courthouse fire. The county was created from portions of Carroll, Sunflower, and Tallahatchie counties, so pre-1871 records are in those parent counties.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by current 1906 building as county grew.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or flood loss at the courthouse
record_losses.description: Leflore County's courthouse has not suffered a major fire or catastrophic flood loss. Records from county formation (1871) survive in generally good condition. The primary research challenge is the county's late formation — for ancestors in this area before 1871, records are in Carroll, Sunflower, or Tallahatchie counties.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Leflore County's records are relatively complete from 1871, but {ancestor_name}'s pre-1871 records must be sought in the parent counties — Carroll County to the east, Sunflower to the west, or Tallahatchie to the north.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Guide to County Records.

deeds.gaps: Pre-1871 deeds in Carroll, Sunflower, or Tallahatchie county records.
deeds.repository: Leflore County Chancery Clerk, Greenwood; MDAH microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1871 estates in parent county records.

marriages.completeness: Good — both white and Black marriages documented from formation
marriages.gaps: Pre-1871 marriages in parent county records.
marriages.repository: Leflore County Circuit Clerk; MDAH microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Compliance improved through 1920s. Greenwood urban records better than rural.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health; MDAH

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1880-1940 (county created 1871; first census enumeration as Leflore County, 1880).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1870 census lists this area under Carroll, Sunflower, or Tallahatchie counties — cross-referencing is essential.

substitute_sources.description: The Greenwood Commonwealth has been published since the late 1890s. Contains legal notices, obituaries, and cotton market reports.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Legal notices, estate settlements, and obituaries fill gaps in court records. Cotton market reports contextualize the agricultural economy.
substitute_sources.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History.

substitute_sources.description: Pre-1871 records for this area filed under Carroll and Sunflower County Freedmen's Bureau offices.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Black families. Records filed under parent county names — search Carroll and Sunflower County Bureau records.

narrative_hooks: Leflore County was not created until 1871 — if {ancestor_name} lived in this area before that date, the records are in Carroll, Sunflower, or Tallahatchie counties, and the genealogist must follow the jurisdictional trail backward.

Greenwood's courthouse survived without major fire or flood — a rare advantage in the Delta — which means {ancestor_name}'s post-1871 records are more likely to exist than in many neighboring counties.

The Freedmen's Bureau records for {ancestor_name}'s area are filed under the parent county names (Carroll or Sunflower), not under Leflore, which did not yet exist — a jurisdictional trap that catches researchers who search only by the modern county name.

Leflore County's late formation means the county itself has no antebellum records — the enslaved people who cleared the land, including {ancestor_name}'s family, appear in the records of counties that no longer exist in their original boundaries.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records. Jackson, MS.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (RG 105).
Cobb, James C. The Most Southern Place on Earth: The Mississippi Delta and the Roots of Regional Identity. New York: Oxford University Press, 1992.
